---
filters:
  - jank
---

# Authoring Jank documents

The previous chapter ([Kindly kinds](kinds.qmd)) covered how to control the rendering of a *value*. This chapter covers the knobs *around* `.jank` blocks ‚Äî display attributes (`echo`, `eval`), Janqua's `output=` override, per-block options (timing, sizing, output suppression) ‚Äî and finishes with the document-level Quarto features (math, callouts) that pair with live evaluation.

## Code block attributes

The Quarto-standard `echo` and `eval` attributes go on the **code-block fence** (the opening line of a code block, e.g. `` ```{.clojure .jank echo=false} ``):

| Attribute | Effect |
|:----------|:-------|
| `echo=false` | Hide the source code, show only the result |
| `eval=false` | Show code without evaluating it |

```{.clojure .jank echo=false}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div "Only the rendered HTML appears ‚Äî no source code shown"]
```

Janqua also recognizes an `output=` attribute that picks a renderer when the value isn't tagged with Kindly metadata:

| Attribute | Effect |
|:----------|:-------|
| `output=html` | Render the value as raw HTML |
| `output=markdown` | Parse the value as markdown |
| `output=hidden` | Suppress both source and result |

When both `:kindly/kind` metadata on the value and `output=` on the fence are set, the Kindly metadata wins.

See the [Quarto documentation on code cells](https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/) for more on the standard attributes.

## Per-block options

These are Janqua-specific knobs ‚Äî not part of the Kindly convention.

| Option | Type | Default | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `timeout` | integer (ms) | `10000` | Per-block eval timeout. |
| `hide-stdout` | boolean | `false` | Suppress captured stdout while keeping the return value. |
| `width` | integer (px) | varies | Chart width. ECharts and Cytoscape need a sized container, so they default to `600`; other chart kinds auto-fit when unset. |
| `height` | integer (px) | varies | Chart height. ECharts and Cytoscape default to `400`; other chart kinds auto-fit when unset. |

`timeout` and `hide-stdout` are read from two places: a fence attribute on the individual block, or a document-wide default in the **frontmatter** (the YAML header at the top of a `.qmd` file).

`width` and `height` are also read from a third place: the value's `:kindly/options` metadata. Sizing is a layout concern that belongs in Kindly's vocabulary, so library functions can set chart dimensions for callers via `with-meta`.

**Precedence (highest first):** fence attribute ‚Üí `:kindly/options` (`width`/`height` only) ‚Üí frontmatter ‚Üí built-in default.

### Fence attribute

Set on the opening line of a code block:

````
```{.clojure .jank timeout=30000 hide-stdout=true}
(do (println "noisy debug")
    (Thread/sleep 100)
    42)
```
````

### Frontmatter

Doc-wide defaults go in the YAML header:

```yaml
---
filters:
  - jank
jank:
  timeout: 30000
  hide-stdout: true
---
```

### `:kindly/options` (width and height only)

Annotate the value's metadata under `:kindly/options`:

```{.clojure .jank}
^{:kindly/kind :kind/plotly
  :kindly/options {:width 800 :height 500}}
{:data [{:x [1 2 3] :y [10 20 15] :type "bar"}]
 :layout {:title "Sized via :kindly/options"}}
```

Library functions can attach these via `with-meta` / `vary-meta`, so callers don't need to set them at the call site.

## Document-level Quarto features

Quarto's authoring features apply to a `.qmd` file the same way they would in any Quarto document ‚Äî Janqua only intervenes for `.jank` code blocks. A few worth knowing:

**Math.** Quarto recognizes LaTeX math in prose: write `$\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$` for inline math like $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$, or `$$ ... $$` for a display equation:

$$\int_0^\infty e^{-x^2}\,dx = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

The same Quarto pipeline handles math returned from `^:kind/md` ‚Äî Janqua emits the markdown string into Pandoc's AST and Quarto then processes the math:

```{.clojure .jank}
^:kind/md
["The area of a circle is $A = \\pi r^2$."]
```

**Callouts**, **frontmatter** (title, theme, format options), **cross-references**, and **citations** are all standard Quarto. See the [Quarto authoring guide](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/) for the full set.
